coredata.dc:title: A novel ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au core–shell metal organic framework nanocomposite as a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for nitrite determination
coredata.dc:creator: Saeb, Elhameh
Asadpour-Zeynali, Karim
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemical sensor has been introduced in the present work. The proposed sensor is a nanocomposite of imidazole zeolites and gold metal nanoparticles that was easily designed and synthesized (core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs). This sensor was used to determine nitrite using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. Nanocomposites have been characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), ultraviolet spectroscopy, scattered X-ray spectroscopy Energy (EDX) and electrochemical element maps and electrochemical techniques. After the plotted the calibration diagram with the differential pulse voltammetry technique, the detection limit was 2 nM and the linear range was obtained 0.64×10−8-22.14×10−3 M for nitrite oxidation. Therefore the method is simple, sensitive, and specific, with a short analysis time, and the main advantages of this purpose sensor are the easy synthesis method, low limit of detection, and good selectivity, excellent stability, reproducibility, and good repeatability in nitrite determination. One of the successes of this sensor is in determining nitrite in water, sausage, and deli meat samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Nitrire
Differential pulse voltammetry
Core-Shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of nitrite on the proposed sensor
Determination the actual area of the nitrite sensor
Calculation of diffusion coefficient for nitrite
The reproducibility, repeatability and stability of proposed sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM image of ZIF-8 (a,b), ZIF-67 (c,d), ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (e,f), ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NP/GCE with a magnification of 500 nm (g), TEM image of ZIF-8@ZIF-67(h,i) with a magnification of 50 nm and 100 nm.

CVs (A) and Nyquist plots (B) recorded at bare GCE (a), ZIF-8@ZIF-67/GCE (b), and ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE (c) in a 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4− and CVs (C) in the 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH=7.0) using of ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE in the absence of nitrite (a), bare GCE (b), ZIF-8@ZIF-67/GCE (c), ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE (d) in the present of nitrite; Scan rate: 50 mV s−1.

(A) CVs responses of ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at scan rates (inner to outer) of 5, 10, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 200, and 300 mV s−1. (B) Plots of peak currents vs. the square root of scan rate. (C) Plots of peak currents vs. of scan rate. (D) Ip/ν1/2 vs. ν. (E) Variation of peak potential vs. log υ. (F) Variation of log E vs. log I, And (G) Variation of log I vs. peak potential (Tafel plot).
(A) Differential pulse voltammetry of nitrite concentrations and (B) calibration plot with a linear range of 0.64×10−8-22.14×10−3 M of nitrite in 0.1 mol L–1 phosphate buffer (pH 7).
Determination of nitrite in water, sausage and deli meat samples (n = 3).
Tolerance of interferences on the determination of nitrite.

head.ce:title: A novel ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au core–shell metal organic framework nanocomposite as a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor for nitrite determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bPharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author: K. Asadpour-Zeynali, Tel.: +98 413 3393113; Fax: +98 413 3340191

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel electrochemical sensor has been introduced in the present work. The proposed sensor is a nanocomposite of imidazole zeolites and gold metal nanoparticles that was easily designed and synthesized (core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs). This sensor was used to determine nitrite using the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique. Nanocomposites have been characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform (FT-IR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), ultraviolet spectroscopy, scattered X-ray spectroscopy Energy (EDX) and electrochemical element maps and electrochemical techniques. After the plotted the calibration diagram with the differential pulse voltammetry technique, the detection limit was 2 nM and the linear range was obtained 0.64×10−8-22.14×10−3 M for nitrite oxidation. Therefore the method is simple, sensitive, and specific, with a short analysis time, and the main advantages of this purpose sensor are the easy synthesis method, low limit of detection, and good selectivity, excellent stability, reproducibility, and good repeatability in nitrite determination. One of the successes of this sensor is in determining nitrite in water, sausage, and deli meat samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, one of the global challenges is protecting human health and the environment. Many pollutants enter the environment daily by humans, one of which is nitrite. Widely used in pharmaceutical, agricultural, and food industries. Sodium nitrite (NaNO2) is a strong oxidizer and a highly reactive substance. [1,2]. Nitrite is a toxic anion that can participate in various chemical reactions. Because nitrite is used as a preservative in meat foods and therapeutic due to its antimicrobial properties and as an enhancer in some foods to improve color and taste, it is found in physiological, environmental, and food systems. It is found in vegetables and water that through the natural cycle of nitrogen from nitrate fertilizers enters vegetables and water sources that are harmful to the human body and cause diseases such as cancer. [2–4]. Children are more at risk than adults. This harmful substance can enter the human body in various ways, one of which is drinking water containing nitrite. Therefore, to control the quality of water and food, quantitative monitoring should be performed to determine the concentration of nitrite in drinking water and food products.[5,6].

Nitrite can have destructive effects through two mechanisms. One is that too much nitrite in the blood can combine with the pigments in the blood to produce methemoglobin, in which oxygen no longer reaches the tissues, which actually leads to hypoxia. Also, they may react with amides and amines in the stomach to form the highly carcinogenic compound N-nitrosamine. [2,4,7-9]. Therefore, making a very sensitive and rapid detection and very selective and suitable to determine nitrite is very important for the management and public health and control of contamination. Various analytical techniques including chromatography [10], spectrophotometry [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], electrochemiluminescence [13], chemiluminescence [14], and electrochemical methods [5] have been used to determine nitrite. Electrochemical methods have advantages over other methods because they are more sensitive, easy operation, selective, faster, simpler, and lower cost. Also, this method uses non-toxic substances and solvents. But many of the methods mentioned above require additional steps, such as removing the matrix, which is time-consuming in the analysis process.

Electrochemical determination of nitrite on the electrode surface is done by oxidation. Since nitrite oxidation is performed on a bare electrode at relatively high potentials. Therefore, the modification of electrodes using suitable materials that easily detect and determine nitrite has made good progress in recent years. Modified materials on the surface of the electrodes can increase the sensitivity and selectivity and can also reduce the additional NO2 ¯ potential compared to the bare electrode and achieve good results. Since the determination of nitrite using the proposed sensor (ZIF-8@ZIF-67@Au NPs) has not been reported, we were able to obtain good results compared to previous work with this sensor. The considered modifier in this work is the core-shell of the zeolite of imidazoles (ZIF-8@ZIF-67), which exerts a better electrocatalytic behavior than individual zeolites because of the synergistic effects.

In recent years, researchers have used more MOFs in gas separation and storage. These compounds are composed of two main components, which are: (1) a metal ion or cluster of metal ions and (2) an organic ligand called a linker. [15–17]. Organic bonding units are usually one-, two-, three-, or four-valent ligands capable of bonding from one to several sides. Of the normal subgroup MOF, imidazole zeolites (ZIFs) are rich in carbon, nitrogen, and transition metals (e.g., Co2+, Zn2+). Synthesis of ZIFs can be easily, cheaply and shows exceptional thermal and chemical stability [16,18]. In fact, this zeolite imidazolate frameworks combine the properties of both zeolites and MOFs. The characteristics of core-shell include high thermal stability, large pore size, large surface area, and remarkable chemical stability [19]. However, the conductivity of core-shell zeolites is poor and it has been suggested that conductive materials enter the MOF structure. Therefore metal nanoparticles are used in modified electrodes due to their wonderful conductivity, electrocatalytic activity, and good chemical stability. In this paper, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were used the modified core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67 electrode to improve conductivity.

Since the determination of nitrite has not been done using the proposed sensor. In this article, we propose a new, sensitive, selectable, fast, and low-cost sensor for investigation of the electrochemical behavior of nitrite on each electrode (core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs electrode and bare electrode). The better results of the electrode modified with this synthesized nanocomposite are due to the excellent conductivity of gold nanoparticles and the larger surface area of the ZIF-8@ZIF-67 core-shell, which causes nitrite oxidation at lower potentials and higher current response. Therefore, this modified electrode could easily detect nitrite in samples such as sausages, deli meat, as well as drinking water, in less time.

All chemicals used in this study are of high purity. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2.6H2O), 2-Methylimidazole (MeIm, purity 99%), and Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2.6H2O), sodium hydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium nitrite (NaNO2), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), and all the salts used in the investigation of the interferences were purchased from Merck. The supporting electrolyte is for NO2 − determination, a 0.1 mol L–1 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7.0.

Electrochemical measurements for nitrite determination were performed by a Metrohm 757 VA and Sama 500 Electro-Analyzer System (Sama, Iran) equipped with a three-electrode system including a platinum wire as a counter electrode and a modified or bare GCE (Diameter (2 mm)) as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as a reference electrode. (All electrodes are obtained from Azar Electrode Company, Urmia, Iran). The pH was measured with a model 827 Metrohm digital pH meter. TEM images were taken using a transmission electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). XRD patterns of the prepared nanocomposite and the modified electrode were recorded on a Bruker AXS model D500 X-ray powder diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation source, λ=0.154056 nm). UV–Vis spectrum was taken on an Avantes spectrophotometer (Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) spectra were taken on an FTIR 8400 system (Shimadzu, Japan). Raman spectra were performed using a TakRam (model P50C0R10) Raman microscope. Surface morphological images were recorded using TESCAN (model MIRA3). According to the device conditions the following, calibration curves for nitrite have been plotted using the DPV technique. The optimized DPV parameters are as pursue: step potential = 0.005V, modulation time = 0.05 s, modulation amplitude = 0.025V, quiet time = 5 s and interval time = 0.5.

For the synthesis of ZIF-8, the first 0.292 g of Zn(NO3)2.6H2O and 0.328 g of 2-methylimidazole (MeIm) were dissolved separately in 25 ml of methanol. The two methanol solutions were mixed together. And continuously, were stirred for half an hour. Then it was static for 24 hours. After 24 hours, ZIF-8 crystals were collected by centrifugation. And after washing with ethanol, the white precipitate was dried overnight at 60 °C and a white powder was obtained. ZIF-8 synthesis was performed for comparison characterization of structure.

For the Preparation of ZIF-67, the first 0.292 g of Co(NO3)2.6H2O and 0.66 g of 2-methylimidazole (MeIm) were dissolved separately in 15 ml of methanol. Then 2-methylimidazole methanol solution was added to cobalt nitrate hexahydrate methanol solution under stirring conditions. After stirring for half an hour, it was placed in a static state for 24 hours. After collection and washing with methanol, the purple precipitate dried overnight at 60 °C. ZIF-67 synthesis was performed for comparison characterization of structure.

First, about 30 mg of ZIF-8 Synthesized was dissolved in 10 ml of 0.5 mM cobalt nitrate hexahydrate methanol solution under stirring for half an hour. After half an hour, 10 ml of 16 mM 2-MeIm methanol solution was added to the first solution under ultrasonic for one hour, and the precipitate formed was centrifuged. After washing, it was dried at 60 °C for 10 hours. Finally, a light purple powder was obtained.

After polishing the surface of the glassy carbon electrode with alumina slurry and rinsing it with water the electrode was placed under ultrasonic in a mixture of water and ethanol for 10 minutes. Finally, 5 µl of suspension was drop onto the cleaned GCE surface and then the electrode was dried to obtain ZIF-8@ZIF-67/GCE. After the electrode was dried, for electrodeposition of Au NPs, by cyclic voltammetry, a potential window of 0.5 to 1 V and a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 for 20 cycles was applied into the 5 mM solution of chloroauric acid. And the modified electrode was obtained with ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE.

In order to extract nitrite ions from sausages and deli meat, a certain amount of crushed samples was first placed in deionized water at 70 °C under stirring for 10 minutes, and finally, the remaining liquid was filtered. However, no preparation was required for the water sample [7,20].
The synthesis process of core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67 structure was performed using ZIF-8 crystals as seeds. In Section S.1 and Figure S1 show the synthesis process.

The structures of the synthesized samples and the modified electrode were investigated using FE-SEM. SEM and TEM images (Fig. 1 a-i) show that the synthesized samples are nanoscale crystals that are approximately rhombic dodecahedrons. The particle sizes are ZIF-8 (78-96 nm), ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (57-72 nm) and ZIF-67 (303 nm) [21–23]. In addition, the white color in Fig. 1g, with a magnification of 500 nm, indicates gold nanoparticles that have been electrochemically precipitated. These nanoparticles actually surrounded the core-shell crystals.

The EDS mapping images in Section S.2 and Figure S2 (a-c) show the uniform distribution of N and C. While Zn is mostly in the center and Co is around the Zn cluster. This indicates that the core-shell has been synthesized. Figure S2, d shows the EDS mapping images from the modified electrode with nanocomposite. In this figure, it can be seen that gold nanoparticles also have a uniform distribution on the electrode surface. XRD of synthesized crystals able a better perception of the core-shell synthesis. The peaks of ZIF-8 are at 7.38 ˚, 10.42 ˚, 12.77 ˚, 14.75 ˚, 16.50 ˚, 18.08 ˚, and 19.53 ˚, respectively show (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222) and (123). The presence of well-formed peaks, especially a very intense peak at 2θ from 7.38 ˚, indicates the highly crystalline formation of ZIF-8 [24–26]. And ZIF-67 pattern respectively (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (233), (134), (044), (244), and (235) which corresponds to the simulated patterns (Fig. S2e) [27]. And crystals of the core-shell (figure S2, e) show the same topological information as ZIF-67 and ZIF-8 crystals. Figure S2, f shows both the core-shell peaks of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 and the peaks of 38.6˚, 44.8˚, 64.9˚ related to (110), (200), and (220). Which represent gold nanoparticles. It has been deposited on the core-shell crystals of ZIF-8@ZIF-67 (JCPDS Card No. 04-0784).

In order to study and compare the spectrum of EDX and EDS mapping images related to ZIF-67 and ZIF-8 it has been evaluated and the relevant results are given in supplementation (section S.3 and Fig. S3). The composition of the elements on the surface of the modified electrode and ZIF-8@ZIF-67 were investigated by X-ray energy scattering spectroscopy (Figure S4, a, b). Figure S4, a relates to the EDX spectrum of a synthesized core-shell sample, in which carbon, cobalt, and zinc atoms are observed, and Figure S4, b is taken from a modified electrode with nanocomposite, which in addition to the mentioned atoms, gold is also seen here, which indicates that the synthesis was successful.

The FT-IR spectrum for the ligand and the synthesized samples were plotted in the range between 400 and 4000 cm−1 (Figure S4, c). As shown in the Figure S4, c for 2-methyl imidazole shows a strong and wideband in the range between 3400 to 2200 cm−1 that binds to the hydrogen bond N ̶ H•••N between two ligands and N-H stretching vibrations in 1846 cm−1 is allocated. These two absorption bands are lost in the synthesized ZIF samples, which could indicate the proton loss of the N-H groups of 2-methyl imidazole ligands after binding to metal ions. Also, the M-N stretching mode has an absorption band of 421 cm−1. This band is present in all three frameworks, which is completely consistent with previous reports [23,28-30]. Hence, the FT-IR results show that zinc and cobalt are present simultaneously in the core-shell framework.

One of the methods that well shows the constitution of the core-shell is the use of UV−vis absorption spectrum (Figure S4, d). The special bands of Co-MeIm tetrahedral at ∼580 and ∼540 nm, which are also observed without significant change in the core-shell framework, represent cobalt in the structure of the core-shell framework [31].

To study the electrochemical behavior of the proposed sensor, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were used. The results of both techniques were completely consistent. To plot CVs, a redox probe solution with a scan rate of 50 mV−1 was used for bare GCE, ZIF-8@ZIF-67/GCE, and ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE (Fig. 2 A). In curve a, which is related to the bare electrode, the increase of peak separation potential (ΔEp) and current is less. But by modifying the electrode, the peak separation potential is reduced, but again, the current is less (curve b). In curve c, by modifying the surface of the electrode with ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs, the peak separation potential decreases, and the peak oxidation current increases. This is due to the presence of gold nanoparticles, which indicates a higher electronic conductivity of the modified electrode, which improves electron transfer. The EIS technique is used to study the surface charge transfer rate and electron transfer properties of the modified electrodes (Fig. 2B). Description of the Nyquist EIS spectrum is given in the reference [32]. The Nyquist diagrams of the three electrodes were plotted in the same solution, which to plot the CV diagrams was used. Since the resistance of electron transfer is determined by the diameter of the semicircle. In curve (a) for the bare electrode, the electron transfer resistance (Rct) is approximately 4800 Ω. By modifying the electrode with ZIF-8@ZIF-67, the diameter of the semicircle is reduced, indicating a decrease in electron resistance. This value is about 680 Ω. This decrease in electron resistance can be due to the increase in the surface area of the electrode and thus increase the charge transfer rate (curve b). By modifying the surface of the electrode with ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs, the diameter semicircular is greatly reduced and the resistance value of the electron is about 350 Ω (curve c). This indicates improved conductivity and thus facilitates the transfer of electrons between the modified electrode surface and the solution. This is due to the presence of gold metal nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of nitrite on the proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the electrochemical behavior of nitrite on the three electrodes mentioned above in the absence and presence of nitrite, the voltammogram cycle was recorded. The recorded cycles for all three electrodes were compared. As shown in Fig. 2C (curve a), no peaks were observed for the ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE modified electrode in the absence of nitrite. By adding nitrite to the buffer for the bare electrode, a peak at 1.1 volts appeared (curve b). By modifying the electrode with a core-shell framework, oxidation related to nitrite has appeared at 0.99 volts (curve c), and also by modifying the electrode with core-shell framework and gold nanoparticles a peak at 0.77 volts has appeared (curve d). Which causes the irreversible oxidation peak displacement of nitrite of about 220 mV compared to the core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/GCE modified electrode. Also, the oxidation current of nitrite in the electrode modified with core-shell framework and gold nanoparticles is higher than other electrodes and sharper peaks have appeared. This can be attributed to the core-shell framework, which increases the surface area of the electrode, and the increase in conductivity of the electrode surface is due to the presence of gold metal nanoparticles. As a result, it converts nitrite to nitrate according to the following reaction (Eq. (1)):
(1)
N
O 2 −
+
H 2
O ⇌ N
O 3 −
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e −

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination the actual area of the nitrite sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The actual area of the proposed sensor for nitrite measurement should be calculated. For this calculate, the cyclic voltammetry method and an oxidation probe were used (in Section S.5 and Figure S5). And finally, the
I p
-
ν
1 / 2
diagram is drawn in Figure S5. According to the Randall-Sausske equation, the microscopic surface of the desired sensor can be calculated from the slope value
I p
-
ν
1 / 2
. According to the slope of the diagram and the Randall-Sausske equation [32], the electrochemically active surface area for the proposed sensor was 0.121 cm2. It can be concluded that the proposed modifier increased the surface area of the electrode by about 4 times compared to the bare electrode.

The pH of solution nitrite, an important factor influencing the peak current and oxidation potential. To optimize this important factor, cyclic voltammetry was recorded using the proposed sensor in the pH range of 3.0-9.0 in a phosphate buffer solution. As can be seen in Section S.6 and figure S6, the peak current responses of nitrite oxidation are regularly increasing in the pH range of 3-7, until it reaches its maximum in pH 7.0. The reason for the low peak current at pHs less than 6.0 is that due to pKa=3.3 for HNO2, and as a result, they are unstable in an acidic media and decompose to NO, NO3 − (Eq. (2):
(2)
2
H +
+ 3 N
O 2 −
→ 2 N O + N
O 3 −
+
H 2
O

At pHs above 7.0, the nitrite peak current response is reduced, which may be due to a lack of protons in the solution, which makes nitrite oxidation difficult. Therefore, the optimal pH is the same as pH 7.0, which is consistent with other previous works. In addition, these studies show that at pH above 7.0, the peak potential remains almost unchanged. But the oxidation potentials of nitrite shift to more negative potentials with increasing pH (3.0-7.0). This indicates the strong dependence of our proposed modifier on the supported electrolyte pH.

Studying the scan rate with the modified electrode indicates the diffusion or adsorption of the electrode process. To do this, cyclic voltammetry was plotted in a solution of nitrite-containing phosphate buffer at different scan rates of 5 to 300 mV s−1. As shown in fig. 3 , the peak anodic nitrite current on the roposed sensor with square root scan rate increases linearly. Which is proportional to the equation of
I p
(
μ A
)
= 1.77
ν
1 2
(
m
V
1 2
s
−
1 2
)
+ 8.25
(
R 2
= 0.9994
)
.

These findings indicate that the nitrite oxidation process on the modified electrode is a diffusion-controlled process that is consistent with previous works [33,34]. The results show that the oxidation of nitrite is irreversible. By plotting
I p
/
ν
1 / 2
versus ν, it is shown that the nitrite electrooxidation process on the proposed sensor is an EC process (Fig. 3D). The mechanism for nitrite can be as follows.
(3)
N
O 2 −
+
H +
⇌ H N
O 2
(4)
N
O 2 −
→ N
O 2
+
e −
(5)
2 N
O 2
+
H 2
O → N
O 2 −
+ N
O 3 −
+ 2
H +
Which in the first stage causes the formation of NO2.

In Fig. 3A, as the scanning rate increases, the oxidation potential of nitrite has shifted to more positive potentials. Fig. 3E shows the linear relationship between potential and logarithm of scan rate. Kinetic parameters can be calculated from the Tafel diagram (Fig. 3G), which is drawn from the sloping part of the cycle voltammogram at the lowest scanning rate. From the Tafel equation, which is the relationship between current and potential for an anode branch [1,32], the α value can be obtained assuming the transfer of one electron in the rate-limiting step to 0.37.

Also, in the scan rates studies, by drawing the diagram of log Epa versus log Ipa (Fig. 3F), a linear relationship between the two was presented and the regression equation was:
log
E
p a
( V )
= 0.0725 log
I
p a
(
μ A
)
− 0.1961
(
R 2
= 0.9808
)

Since with increasing scan rate, a significant change in the displacement of nitrite oxidation potential to more anodic potentials occurs, so in high rate scanning, nitrite oxidation becomes more difficult [34,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of diffusion coefficient for nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: A chronoamperometry experiment was performed to obtain the diffusion coefficient of the nitrite with the modified electrode. To perform this test, the potential of the electrode was set at a constant value of 0.9 V, which was recorded for 60 s at different concentrations of nitrite in this test (Figure S7 in the section S.7). Cottrellian behavior for nitrite was observed with the modified electrode [32]. By plotting the graphs and using the Cottrell equation, the diffusion coefficient for nitrite was 1.35×10−5, which is consistent with the previous works [36,37].

One of the important measuring parameters is determining the linear range of analyte concentration. The DPV technique was used to plot the calibration diagram in optimal conditions. In fig. 4 A, with increasing nitrite concentration, the peak current is also increasing regularly. From plotting the current peak versus concentration, a calibration diagram was obtained. According to Fig. 4 B, the current peak is in the range of 0.64×10−8-22.14×10−3 M, in proportion to the nitrite concentration. Under the following equation.
y = 16.898 C + 1.559
(
R 2
= 0.999
)

Quantitative limit (LOQ) and detection limit (LOD) were calculated using the formula, LOQ = 10 SD/m and LOD = 3 SD/m (m is the slope of the calibration diagram and SD is the standard deviation of the lowest concentration in the linear range). LOQ and LOD were calculated to be 6.66 nM and 2 nM, respectively. The performance of the different sensors with different electrodes reported in Table S1 is given. As can be seen, the detection limit and LDR of the proposed sensor are better than that of other modifiers of the reported electrodes. Using the calibration diagram, nitrite was measured in real samples.

The application of the proposed sensor for nitrite in real samples was investigated. To do this, a sample of river water without any pretreatment was used to measure the concentration of nitrite using the DPV technique in the same optimal conditions as the calibration curve was drawn. The standard increase method was used to find the nitrite concentration. The accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated by increasing a certain amount of standard nitrite solution in the river water sample and then measuring the nitrite concentration and comparing the results with the expected amount. For sausage and deli meat samples, the preparation of which is given in the experimental section, the standard increase method was used and the nitrite concentration in sausages was obtained. The results of this section are given in Table 1 . Therefore, core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE can be successfully used to determine nitrite in water, sausage and deli meat samples using direct protocols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reproducibility, repeatability and stability of proposed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry was used to evaluate the reproducibility, stability, and repeatability of the proposed sensor. To evaluate the stability of the sensor, the prepared electrode was kept at room temperature and cyclic voltammetry was plotted at different time intervals. Finally, the current response maintained its initial value of 97% for one week and 92% for one month. Also, the repeatability of the proposed sensor response was investigated by performing 5 repetitive tests on one electrode, which obtained an RSD of 1.45%, which indicates the repeatability of the response proposed sensor. Finally, in order to know of reproducibility the proposed sensor, the current response of the five modified electrodes prepared in the same method to nitrite was plotted and the calculated RSD was obtained to be 3.5%. Which indicates that the proposed sensor has good reproducibility. Therefore, the above results show that the proposed sensor for nitrite electrooxidation has good stability, repeatability, and reproducibility. The corresponding results are shown in Section S.8 and Figure S8.

Selectivity is an important factor for sensors. As can be seen in the definition of selectivity, ie the sensor's response to the desired analyte is among the species or sample matrix. This sensor is not only very sensitive but also has good selectivity. So to evaluate the selectivity of this sensor, different concentrations of some cations, anions, and molecules such as Cl−, Br−, NO3 −,SO4 2−, Na+, K+, Mg2+, glucose, Ascorbic acid (AA) was added separately to the nitrite-containing buffer solution. According to the definition of tolerance level, the maximum concentration of foreign species that changes the analytical response of the analysis is less than 5%. The results of this section are given in Table 2 . As can be seen, 300-fold Na+, K+, Cl−, NO3 −, 800-fold Br−, 1000-fold Mg2+, SO4 2−, 10-fold glucose, and 5-fold ascorbic acid only minor changes are made in the DPV nitrite current response. From these results, it is clear that the proposed sensor is capable of detecting nitrite in environmental contaminants.

Core-shell ZIF-8@ZIF-67/Au NPs/GCE was used as a new electrochemical sensor to determine nitrite in this research work. This sensor is a combination of chemical and electrochemical synthesis that was provided fast, easy, and low-cost. This sensor has a good catalytic effect for the nitrite oxidation reaction due to its high porosity and increased electrode surface area, good conductivity and, electron transfer between the electrode and the nitrite surface. The nitrite oxidation signal was significantly improved using the modified electrode compared to the bare electrode. Our proposed sensor also has advantages such as significant stability, simplicity and ease of sensor preparation, signal repeatability, high sensitivity, and low cost. The use of this sensor to measure nitrite in real samples has facilitated it due to its good selectivity, wide linear range, high electrochemical performance, very low detection limit, and biocompatibility.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the financial support from the University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140278.
